coredata.dc:title: Au nanoparticles and graphene quantum dots co-modified glassy carbon electrode for catechol sensing
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2016 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Zhao, Xuan
He, Dawei
Wang, Yongsheng
Hu, Yin
Fu, Chen
coredata.dc:description: In this letter, the gold nanoparticles and graphene quantum dots were applied to the modification of glassy carbon electrode for the detection of catechol. The synergist cooperation between gold nanoparticles and graphene quantum dots can increase specific surface area and enhance electronic and catalytic properties of glassy carbon electrode. The detection limit of catechol is 0.869μmol/L, demonstrating the superior detection efficiency of the gold nanoparticles and graphene quantum dots co-modified glassy carbon electrode as a new sensing platform.

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of the Au-GQDs modified GCEs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Beijing Council of Science and Technology

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support from the Chinese Natural Science Fund Project ( 61335006 , 61527817 and 61378073 ), and Beijing Science and Technology Committee ( Z151100003315006 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of GQDs (a), AuNPs (b), and Au-GQDs (c), respectively. Corresponding size distributions of GQDs (d), AuNPs (e), and Au-GQDs (f), respectively.
(a) UV–vis absorption spectra of Au, GQDs and Au-GQDs. (b) Full scan XPS spectra of GQDs. (c) The C1s XPS spectrum of GQDs. (d) The O1s XPS spectrum of GQDs.

(a) Cyclic voltammetry curves of 50μM catechol at bare GCE, Au/GCE, GQDs/GCE and Au-GQDs modified GCE. (b) Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy of bare GCE and AuNPs/GCE for the Faradaic impedance measurements.

(a) Cyclic voltammograms of 50μM catechol at Au-GQDs/GCE with different scan rates. (b) The peak current at 0.237V of catechol vs. the sqrt of scan rate. (c) Differential pulse voltammograms of different concentrations of catechol at Au-GQDs modified GCE. The pulse period: 0.2s, amplitude: 50mV. (d) The calibration curve between concentration and the current of peak based on DPV.
The changes of Au-CQDs/GCE activity as 0.1M Na+, K+, Cl−, SO4 2−, carbamide, citric acid and glucose added into the solution when detected 50μM catechol.

head.ce:title: Au nanoparticles and graphene quantum dots co-modified glassy carbon electrode for catechol sensing

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Luminescence and Optical Information, Beijing Jiaotong University, No. 3 Shang Yuan Cun, Hai Dian District, Beijing CN 100044, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Luminescence and Optical Information, Beijing Jiaotong University
sa:affiliation.sa:address-line: No. 3 Shang Yuan Cun, Hai Dian District

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The Au-GQDs were applied to the modification of GCE for detection of catechol. The synergist material effectively improved the electronic and catalytic properties of GCE. As a result, the catechols were effectively detected with the new sensing platform. This method is promising for the detection of catechol in environmental monitoring and safety assessment.

In this letter, the gold nanoparticles and graphene quantum dots were applied to the modification of glassy carbon electrode for the detection of catechol. The synergist cooperation between gold nanoparticles and graphene quantum dots can increase specific surface area and enhance electronic and catalytic properties of glassy carbon electrode. The detection limit of catechol is 0.869μmol/L, demonstrating the superior detection efficiency of the gold nanoparticles and graphene quantum dots co-modified glassy carbon electrode as a new sensing platform.

ce:section.ce:para: As a typical phenol derivative, catechol is widely used in various fields: medicine, photographic developer and hair dyes [1]. Catechol often coexists in environmental samples as pollutants with high toxicity and low degradation rate. It is very harmful to human body due to its biotoxicity and irritation to skin [2]. A myriad of methods have been exploited to meet the rising demands for the determination of catechol, including gas chromatography [3], chemiluminescence [4], mass spectrometry [5], ultraviolet spectrometry [6], which may be expensive and time-consuming. Compared to those methods, the electro-chemical approach has many advantages including time-saving, simplicity and no need for expensive instruments [7].

Gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) have many splendid properties: high conductivity, high specific surface area and superior catalytic [8–10]. It can be combined with a variety of biological macromolecules [11], and does not affect biological activity [12]. Because of the aggregation, AuNPs are inactive without basic supports [13]. To improve the sensitivity of the GEC modified with AuNPs, GQDs are chosen for the supporting materials. Carbon materials have been widely used as the supports, such as carbon nanotubes [14], carbon fibers [15] and graphene [16]. However, these materials may block surface active sites and impede electron transfer [17]. The synthesis of these carbon materials is also high cost and time-consuming [18]. GQDs are quasi-zero-dimensional nanomaterials [19], possessing high electrical conductivity [20], large surface area and biocompatibility [21–23]. Recently, GQDs have many promising applications in resistive sensors [24,25], optical dyes [26–30] and biological medicine [31]. However, to the best of our knowledge, there is no method reported about the detection of catechol based on glassy carbon electrode modified with Au-GQDs. Herein, we proposed a novel and facile approach to detect trace amounts of catechol based on Au-GQDs modified electrodes through cyclic voltammetry (CV) and differential pulse voltammetry (DPV).

All electrochemical measurements were carried out on a CHI660D electrochemical workstation (Chenhua Instrument, Shanghai, China) using a three-electrode system, platinum wire as the counter electrode, and saturated calomel electrode (SCE) as the reference electrode. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were achieved by a JEM-1400 operating at 120kV. The optical absorption spectra of AuNPs, GQDs, and Au-GQDs were obtained by a UV-3101 scanning spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan) at room temperature. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurement was carried on PHI Quantera (PHI, Japan), the binding energy is calibrated with C1s=284.8eV.

GQDs were produced by incompletely pyrolyzing citric acid. Briefly, 0.5g citric acid was added into a 5mL beaker. The beaker was heated at 200°C for 30min. Citric acid liquated rapidly and its color changed to orange red. The liquid was transferred into a 20mL beaker and diluted with 10mL distilled water. After filtration, the sample solution was neutralized to pH 7.0 using dialysis bag (3500Da). Subsequently, the neutral solution was dried by lyophilization.

The gold nanoparticles were synthesized through reducing HAuCl4 with trisodium citrate. Briefly, 500μL HAuCl4 (25.4mM) was added into 40mL boiling distilled water under vigorous stirring, and 900μL trisodium citrate (0.1M) was quickly added into the boiling solution under refluxing for 20min, the color changed to wine red. Finally, the suspension was cooled down to room temperature under continuous stirring and stored at 4°C.

In a typical experiment, AuNPs were combined with GQDs through in situ growth method. Briefly, 3.5mg GQDs was dissolved into 35mL deionized water, 200μL HAuCl4 (25.4mM) solution was dropped into GQDs solution and stirred for 15min. The mixture was heated to boiling under stirring, then 200μL trisodium citrate (0.1M) was added into the boiling mixture quickly. The solution was refluxed for 30min, the color changed to purplish red. After cooling down to room temperature, the solution was dialyzed (3500Da) and stored at 4°C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the Au-GQDs modified GCEs
ce:section.ce:para: Before modification, the GCE was successively polished by 1.0, 0.3 and 0.05μm aluminum slurry for 10min. Each time after using one kind of aluminum slurry, the GCE was sonicated for 1min and rinsed thoroughly with deionized water and ethyl alcohol. The polished GCE was successively sonicated with acetone, ethanol, redistilled water and dried under ambient condition. Au-GQDs solution was drop-casted onto the surface of polished GCE as the modified layer and dried under infrared lamp.

Different volumes of catechol solution (0.364mM) were individually dropped into pH 7.0 B–R buffer (Britton–Robinson buffer). K3Fe(CN)6 (10mM) was used as the redox probe. The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) experiments were carried out with a frequency range from 0.1Hz to 0.1MHz. The applied potential was 0.24V and amplitude was 5mV. Cyclic voltammetry (CV) was recorded from −0.2 to 0.5V under different scan rates in the presence of 50μM catechol. Differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) was performed with different concentrations. The potential was from −0.1 to 0.5V. The amplitude was 50mV and the pulse width was 200ms.

Figure 1(a) and (b) displays TEM images of the GQDs and AuNPs. Due to the abundant negatively charged carboxyl groups, GQDs synthesized from incomplete pyrolyzation of citric acid display high dispersion in solutions. From the TEM characterizations, most of the prepared GQDs were uniformly dispersed and the average size is about 1.5nm. The AuNPs (Figure 1(b)) also show well dispersion without obvious aggregation. The average gold particle size is about 15nm (Figure 1(e)).

As shown in Figure 1(c) Au-GQDs were observed at this image. The size and morphology of Au-GQDs change clearly compare to the single gold particle (Figure 1(b)), that indicating the successful formation of Au-GQDs composite. A measurement of the Au-GQDs in Figure 1(f) indicates that the average size is 24nm.

In addition to that, the success formation of Au-GQDs composite is also evidenced by the ultraviolet visible optical absorption spectrum (UV–vis). Figure 2(a) shows the UV–vis of Au, GQDs and Au-GQDs respectively. The absorption spectrum of GQDs shows an acromion-peak at 340nm. The absorption peak of gold nanoparticle is about at 520nm. This indicates a particle size of 15nm and agrees with observation by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Figure 1(b)). Compared to the single AuNPs, the absorption peak of GQD-AuNPs is red-shifted and broadened, this may result from the clustering of particles through the neutralization of positive charges on AuNP and negatively-charged GQDs. Both UV–vis and TEM suggest the size increase of Au NPs along with GQDs.

To further explore the content and configuration of the GQDs, XPS characterization was carried out. The full range XPS (Figure 2(b)) analysis of the resultant GQDs sample clearly showed the presence of C1s and O1s peaks centered at 284eV and 532eV, respectively. C1s (Figure 2(c)) can be divided into three different peaks, corresponding to the CC (284eV), CO (286eV) and OCO (289eV). O1s (Figure 2(d)) corresponds to the peaks of CO (532.3eV) and CO/COC (533eV).

Figure 3(a) shows CVs of bare GCE, Au/GCE, GQDs/GCE and Au-GQDs/GCE in 50μM catechol solutions. Due to the good electrical conductivity and large specific surface area of Au-GQDs, the modified electrode displays obvious reduction and oxidation peak of catechol, which cannot be found by other electrode. The interface properties of electrode surfaces were performed by EIS with 10mM K3Fe(CN)6 as the redox probe. From Figure 3(b), it can be obtained that in the high frequency range of Nyquist plot, GCE displays no semicircle while Au-GQDs/GCE has a small semicircle diameter, which indicates electron transfer at the interface could be restricted partially due to Au-GQDs film. Compared with the interaction between Au-GQDs and catechol, this attenuating effect is negligible in the whole system. These phenomena indicate that Au-GQDs can effectively enhance the sensitivity of GCE for catechol.

The CV curves of 50μM catechol at Au-GQDs/GCE under different scan rates are represented in Figure 4(a). With the increasing scan rates, the reduction and oxidation peak current of catechol increase, while anodic peak potential (Epa) and cathodic peak potential (Epc) remain almost no change indicating the fast electron transfer kinetics and good electrochemical reaction ability of the Au-GQDs/GCE. Figure 4(b) shows that the oxidation peak currents are proportional to the scan rates (R =0.998), which implies a typical diffusion controlled electrochemical behavior of catechol.

The DPVs are carried out to detect catechol meticulously. As shown in Figure 4(c), oxidation peak currents of catechol increase with the gradual addition of concentrations. There is a typical linear relationship between oxidation peak currents and concentration with a correlation coefficient of 0.996 (Figure 4(d)). The detection range is from 2μM to 50μM and the limit of detection is 0.869μM, indicating this proposed method with successfully modified Au-GQDs/GCE has advantages such as low cost, time-saving, low detection limit and wide linear range.

To test the selectivity of catechol detection, we have investigated the effect of some potentially interfering substances (Na+, K+, Cl− SO4 2−, carbamide, citric acid and glucose) on the Au-GQDs/GCE. The interferences with the concentration of 0.1M, which are 2000 times greater than that of catechol (50μM), were added into the solution of catechol. Figure 5 shows that Na+, K+, Cl−, SO4 2− and carbamide caused negligible influence on peak current of catechol, citric acid and glucose have few interferences with catechol detection. This result demonstrates that the present method shows better selectivity for catechol over other potential interferences.

In this letter, AuNPs were combined with GQDs through in situ growth method. The Au-GQDs were applied to the modification of GCE for detection of catechol. The linear range is from 2μM to 50μM and the limit of detection is 0.869μM. This simple sensing platform of Au-GQDs modified GCE is promising for the detection of catechol in environmental monitoring and safety assessment.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support from the Chinese Natural Science Fund Project (61335006, 61527817 and 61378073), and Beijing Science and Technology Committee (Z151100003315006).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Spectrochim. Acta A: Mol. Biomol. Spectrosc.
Spectrochim. Acta A: Mol. Biomol. Spectrosc.
